title: Conagra Brands · Production Line Scheduling
subtitle: MIT Sloan Capstone · Data Science Capstone Intern
highlights: Building a production-line scheduling tool that helps plant managers minimize equipment downtime and changeover costs across a multi-line manufacturing facility
Establishing a scalable blueprint for company-wide data standardization across Databricks, SAP, and Power BI so the same scheduling pipeline can extend to additional plants
Integrating an agentic AI layer that responds to unexpected events (machine outages, ingredient shortages, demand spikes) and proposes real-time schedule adjustments to the human operator
Working directly with Conagra plant managers, MIT Sloan faculty advisor Bruce Lawler, and Conagra's analytics leadership team
description: MIT Sloan Master of Business Analytics capstone with Conagra Brands as a Data Science Capstone Intern. The project pairs an optimization-driven scheduling tool with an agentic AI layer for real-time disruption response, and lays the groundwork for company-wide data standardization. Detailed deliverables are confidential under the program's industry charter, so this entry stays at a high level.

title: Optimization · Water Access in the DRC
subtitle: MIT Sloan · Optimization Methods · Fall 2025
highlights: Built a two-stage mixed-integer program in Julia / JuMP: pass 1 maximizes covered population subject to a budget; pass 2 minimizes average walking distance while preserving pass-1 coverage
Combined a WorldPop population raster (clipped to the DRC) with the Water Point Data Exchange (WPDx) functional / non-functional well dataset
Compared a uniform square demand grid against a population-weighted K-means grid (K = 100,000); K-means preserved more population and quadrupled neighbor density at a feasible compute cost
Generated 3,000 candidate well locations by ranking demand cells by unserved population and enforcing a 4.5 km minimum spacing to avoid clustering
Improved projected access to water by 19% across budget scenarios from $0M to $30M
Joint work with César Dori
description: MIT Sloan Optimization Methods project applying a two-stage mixed-integer program to humanitarian infrastructure planning. The model selects which existing non-functional wells to repair and where to place new wells across the Democratic Republic of Congo to maximize population reached and minimize walking distance, subject to budget and geographic constraints. Pipelines were built to scale across very large candidate-site sets and to compare multiple budget and grid-construction scenarios.

title: Predictive + Prescriptive Solar Battery Strategy
subtitle: MIT Sloan · 15.095 Machine Learning Under a Modern Optimization Lens · Spring 2026
highlights: Built a predictive-to-prescriptive framework for a solar-paired battery in the SDG&E (San Diego Gas & Electric) market
Predictive layer: forecasted next-day solar generation from NSRDB irradiance / temperature data and forecasted SDG&E day-ahead Locational Marginal Prices

Prescriptive layer: an optimization in Julia that prescribes hourly charge / hold / discharge actions subject to physical battery constraints (state-of-charge limits, charge/discharge rates, no grid charging)

Implemented two robust formulations to handle price uncertainty: an ellipsoidal uncertainty set with a second-order cone penalty (Γₚ = 10) and a budgeted uncertainty set protecting against the worst-case subset of downward price shocks
Increased projected profits by 20% over a rule-based heuristic baseline, with greater stability under realized price deviations
Joint work with Justine Murdock under Prof. Dimitris Bertsimas
description: MIT Sloan 15.095 project that combines predictive modeling and prescriptive optimization to operate a grid-scale solar-paired battery in San Diego. The predictive layer forecasts next-day solar generation and SDG&E day-ahead prices; the prescriptive layer is an optimization in Julia that decides charging, holding, and selling actions to maximize revenue, with both ellipsoidal and budgeted robust formulations to handle price uncertainty. The pipeline demonstrates how ML and optimization can be cleanly chained together end-to-end.

title: Boston Public Health Commission · Shelter Occupancy Forecasting
subtitle: MIT Sloan · Analytics Lab · ML Project Lead · Fall 2025
highlights: Developed 14-day forecasts of daily bed demand for the BPHC's two main shelters: 112 Southampton (men's) and Woods Mullen (women's), spanning 2015–2025 of historical data

Trained a multi-model stack — linear regression for interpretation, LASSO for balance, and ensemble methods (Random Forest, Gradient Boosting, ARIMA) for performance — and engineered lagged T-14 → T relationships so forecasts can be made without future inputs
Engineered features across weather, COVID-era policy regimes (stay-at-home orders, CHNV, Ordinance 1373, CARE housing), and macro housing-market predictors informed by the homelessness literature

Generated pseudo-confidence intervals from cross-validation residuals because ensemble methods don't produce them natively, and ran sensitivity analysis with simulated missing data to stress-test robustness
Improved capacity-planning accuracy by 50% and integrated outputs into a Streamlit decision-support interface for BPHC operations staff
Selected for poster presentation at the 2026 CSTE Annual Conference (Boston, MA), reaching 2,800+ public health professionals
description: MIT Sloan Analytics Lab project with the Boston Public Health Commission. The team built and deployed a suite of machine-learning models — linear, LASSO, Random Forest, Gradient Boosting, and ARIMA — to forecast 14-day occupancy across the city's homeless shelter network, with results integrated into a Streamlit decision-support application for program managers. Feature-importance and interpretability analysis surfaced the social, environmental, and temporal factors driving demand, enabling better resource allocation.

title: Greater Boston · Data Visualization Proof of Concept
subtitle: MIT MBAn · Data Visualization team project
highlights: Interactive proof-of-concept visualization of Greater Boston combining geospatial layers with chart-driven detail panels
Explores how careful chart design and cartographic layering can make a complex regional dataset legible and navigable
Live deployment hosted on GitHub Pages — view the live site for the full experience
Collaborative MIT MBAn data-visualization team project
description: Collaborative MIT data-visualization project exploring Greater Boston through an interactive proof-of-concept that combines geospatial layers with chart-driven detail panels. The project showcases how careful chart design and cartographic layering can make a complex regional dataset legible and explorable.

title: Mean Reversion + Momentum Trading Strategies
subtitle: MIT Sloan · 15.C51 Proprietary Trading · Spring 2026
highlights: Developed and backtested two opposing ML-driven trading strategies — mean reversion and momentum — on 29 DJIA constituents over 2015–2026
Engineered features from technical indicators, cross-sectional return ranks, sector momentum, and calendar signals, then filtered them by Information Coefficient and pairwise redundancy pruning
Trained 120 models across six model families, two strategies, and five holding periods (1, 5, 21, 63, 126 days)
Used purged walk-forward validation with 180-day embargo windows throughout to prevent data leakage from the test set into training
5-day mean-reversion model achieved a Sharpe ratio of 1.34 and CAGR of 19.9% on the test set
63-day momentum model produced consistent positive risk-adjusted returns across validation, test, and embargo periods (Sharpe 1.73 / 0.70 / 3.50)

Identified realized short-term volatility as the most predictive feature across both strategies, and recommended a volatility-conditioned allocation that combines both strategies rather than running either in isolation
description: MIT Sloan 15.C51 Proprietary Trading project that builds and rigorously backtests two opposing strategies — mean reversion and momentum — on DJIA constituents, with strict purged walk-forward validation and 180-day embargo windows to prevent leakage. The two strategies turn out to be regime-complementary, motivating a volatility-conditioned allocation rather than either approach in isolation.

title: Optimization · F1 Race Calendar Redesign
subtitle: UC Berkeley · IEOR 162 · Nonlinear & Discrete Optimization
highlights: Modeled the 2024 Formula 1 calendar as a constrained TSP / sequencing optimization in AMPL, with binary x_{i,j} variables over all 22 race cities (484 city pairs)
Computed Haversine distances between every city pair using a custom R + geosphere preprocessing pipeline that parsed JSON race data into a distance matrix
Compared three approaches: a nearest-neighbor heuristic, a pure minimum-distance LP, and an LP with FIA contract / weather / venue constraints

Original 2024 calendar: 74,713.65 miles; nearest-neighbor: 34,689.94 mi (≈1,066 t CO₂ saved per team); pure minimum-distance LP: 30,090.94 mi (≈1,189 t CO₂); constrained LP: 39,910.02 mi (≈927 t CO₂ saved while still respecting real-world constraints)
Connects directly to the FIA's net-zero-by-2030 commitment, since logistics accounted for 45% of F1's 2019 carbon footprint
description: UC Berkeley IEOR 162 project that re-imagines the 2024 Formula 1 calendar as a constrained routing problem. The AMPL model finds a re-ordering of races that minimizes global travel distance while respecting venue, weather, and contractual constraints, reducing average emissions by an estimated 927 tons of CO₂ per team — connecting directly to the FIA's net-zero-by-2030 commitment.

title: Yelp Sentiment & Star Rating Prediction
subtitle: MIT Sloan · Deep Learning · Fall 2025
highlights: Cast Yelp star-rating prediction as a regression problem from raw review text and trained neural networks at three levels of representation: bag-of-words counts, pretrained GloVe embeddings, and a frozen BERT backbone with a regression head
Built two working datasets (1,000 and 26,318 reviews) with reproducible 80/20 train-test and 90/10 train-val splits to enable apples-to-apples comparison across representations and dataset sizes
Best test performance came from the bag-of-words baseline on the full 26k dataset (MSE 0.58 / MAE 0.60), outperforming GloVe (MSE 0.89) and BERT-on-2.5k (MSE 0.89)
Studied data-efficiency tradeoffs: BoW continued to benefit from additional data while GloVe plateaued near MSE 0.9 and BERT was compute-bound at small subsamples
Built supplementary CV models on review-attached images to classify scenes (food, drink, etc.) and explore image-driven proxies for business performance
description: MIT Sloan Deep Learning project on the Yelp Open Dataset. The work compares three levels of text representation — one-hot bag-of-words, pretrained GloVe embeddings, and a pretrained BERT transformer — and trains neural networks at each level to predict star ratings directly from review text, evaluating performance as a function of representation and training-set size. A supplementary CV pipeline explores Yelp review images.

title: MongoDB vs SQL · Yelp Query Benchmarking
subtitle: UC Berkeley · Data 101 Final Project
highlights: Sampled the Yelp Academic Dataset down to ~50k users, ~50k businesses, and ~1M reviews to fit local PostgreSQL 14 and MongoDB instances
Authored matched analytical queries in both engines — top businesses by state, top reviewers, multi-step aggregations — and benchmarked them with EXPLAIN ANALYZE and MongoDB's own explain plans

Largest gap: Top-Businesses-By-State ran in 354 ms on PostgreSQL versus 46,279 ms on MongoDB, driven by Postgres window functions vs. a MongoDB COLLSCAN over 150,346 documents and a complex $group / $unwind pipeline
Quantified the impact of indexing: indexing on businesses.review_count and businesses.stars cut PostgreSQL to ~300 ms and MongoDB to ~10,000 ms
Concluded with a decision rubric: PostgreSQL for structured, query-intensive analytical workloads; MongoDB for unstructured, dynamic, write-heavy data
description: Data-engineering final project that puts MongoDB and PostgreSQL head-to-head on the Yelp dataset. After loading equivalent samples into both systems, the team authored matched analytical queries and benchmarked them with EXPLAIN ANALYZE, with and without indexing, to inform when each technology is the right tool.

title: Voltage Discharge Prediction · Hydrogen Electrolyzers
subtitle: Verdagy · Data Science Intern · Summer 2024
highlights: Automated Python algorithms to ingest real-time raw sensor streams from 10 lab-scale alkaline electrolyzers, removing noise, outliers, and detected sensor malfunctions
Reduced data-cleaning labor by 80% and improved the accuracy of downstream summary statistics that fed scientist-facing dashboards
Designed and rolled out 9 dashboards in Python and Seeq to track voltage drift and hydrogen production in real time
Enabled faster R&D decision-making for the engineering and executive teams by surfacing degradation patterns under simulated renewable-energy intermittency
description: Industry research at Verdagy on alkaline electrolyzer performance. Built Python pipelines to ingest, denoise, and align multivariate sensor streams from 10 lab-scale electrolyzers, then designed nine dashboards in Python and Seeq for real-time monitoring of voltage drift and hydrogen production — enabling faster R&D decision-making for scientists and executives.

title: Product Analytics · Marketing Kaggle Competition
subtitle: MIT Sloan · Product Analytics · Top 10 leaderboard finish
highlights: Top-10 leaderboard finish in a class-wide marketing-analytics Kaggle competition
Heavy feature engineering on customer-behavior data — recency / frequency / monetary aggregates, interaction terms, and tenure features
Ensembled multiple gradient-boosted models with cross-validated weights to stabilize the leaderboard score
Demonstrates practical product-analytics modeling end-to-end, from raw transactional data to a deployable scoring function
description: Kaggle-style marketing analytics competition where my team placed in the top 10 on the leaderboard. The project involved heavy feature engineering on customer-behavior data and ensembling of gradient-boosted models, included here as evidence of practical product-analytics work.

title: ArcGIS Mapping · ESPM 110
subtitle: UC Berkeley · Environmental health spatial analyses
highlights: Produced map deliverables for UC Berkeley's ESPM 110 (The Politics & Ecology of Environmental Health)

Worked through full GIS workflows — DEM-based hydrologic flow direction around Strawberry Creek, fire-risk overlays of the Berkeley Hills combining slope, road networks, and land-management classifications
Practiced classical cartographic design: legend hierarchy, color-by-class, annotations, and scale bars sized for technical-report inclusion
Final maps are featured on the dedicated Maps page of this site
description: Selected map outputs from coursework in UC Berkeley's ESPM 110 (The Politics & Ecology of Environmental Health). The full reports are coursework, but the map deliverables — included on the dedicated Maps page — showcase hands-on ArcGIS work: data layering, spatial joins, classification, and cartographic design.
